seoTitle: How to Process Credit Card Payments in QuickBooks
metaDescription: Accept and record customer credit card payments natively in QuickBooks Online through the Receive Payment and Intuit Payments flow.
intro: Collecting card payments from clients doesn't have to mean juggling a separate terminal and manually re-entering totals. This guide covers using QuickBooks Online's built-in Receive Payment form with Intuit Payments to charge a card directly and automatically track processing fees. It's for business owners who want customer payments and their books to stay in sync.

steps.heading: Open the Receive Payment form
steps.body: Click + New, then choose Receive Payment under the Customers column.

steps.heading: Select the customer and invoice
steps.body: Choose the client from the dropdown and check the box next to the specific open invoice you're settling.

steps.heading: Choose Credit Card as the method
steps.body: Set the payment method to Credit Card, then click Enter Card Details to open the secure card entry form.

steps.heading: Enter the card details
steps.body: Type in the card number, expiration date, CVV, and billing zip code, and optionally keep the card on file if you have client authorization for recurring billing.

steps.heading: Process the charge and send a receipt
steps.body: Click Process Card, wait for authorization, then Save and Send to email the customer a payment receipt.

faq.question: Do I need separate card processing hardware?
faq.answer: No, QuickBooks' built-in Intuit Payments lets you enter card details manually without a physical terminal.

faq.question: How are processing fees tracked?
faq.answer: QuickBooks automatically deducts merchant service fees behind the scenes, keeping your gross revenue accounts balanced.

faq.question: When will the payment show up in my bank account?
faq.answer: Check your Merchant Service Deposits dashboard to see exactly when the net settled amount posts to your checking register.

faq.question: Is it safe to save a customer's card on file?
faq.answer: Yes, but only do so with explicit client authorization, since it's meant specifically for recurring billing arrangements.
